title: Lovász Conjecture
statement_text: Every connected vertex-transitive graph admits a Hamiltonian path.
context_text: Posed by Lovász in 1969, this is one of the two central open problems on Hamiltonicity in vertex-transitive graphs addressed in this paper. To date only four connected vertex-transitive graphs on at least 3 vertices are known which do not have a Hamiltonian cycle, one of which is the Petersen graph.
paper_contribution: Shows that every connected vertex-transitive graph on $n\geq 3$ vertices contains a cycle (and hence a path) of length at least $\Omega(n^{13/21})$, improving the previous bound of $\Omega(n^{3/5})$ due to DeVos (2023).
notes: Original reference is Lovász [40]: Proc. Calgary Internat. Conf., 1969.
paper_title: Longest cycles in vertex-transitive and highly connected graphs

title: Thomassen Conjecture
statement_text: Apart from finitely many exceptional graphs, all connected vertex-transitive graphs contain a Hamiltonian cycle.
context_text: Conjectured by Thomassen in 1978, this strengthens the Lovász conjecture by asserting Hamiltonicity (rather than merely a Hamiltonian path) for all but finitely many connected vertex-transitive graphs. It is the second of the two central open problems on vertex-transitive Hamiltonicity addressed in this paper.
paper_contribution: Shows that every connected vertex-transitive graph on $n\geq 3$ vertices contains a cycle of length at least $\Omega(n^{13/21})$, improving the previous bound of $\Omega(n^{3/5})$ due to DeVos (2023).
notes: Cited as Conjecture 1 in Barát and Kriesell [6] (2010) with references therein; original formulation 1978.
paper_title: Longest cycles in vertex-transitive and highly connected graphs

title: Conjecture 1.2 (Smith's Conjecture)
statement_text: For all $r\geq 2$, every two longest cycles in an $r$-connected graph meet in at least $r$ vertices.
context_text: This conjecture, commonly attributed to Smith and dating from 1984, has been verified for $r\leq 6$ by Grötschel and for $r\in\{7,8\}$ by Stewart and Thompson, and recently for $r\geq(n+16)/7$ by Gutiérrez and Valqui. The best general lower bound prior to this paper was $\Omega(r^{3/5})$ due to Chen, Faudree and Gould (1998), itself improving on $\sqrt{r}-1$ by Burr and Zamfirescu.
paper_contribution: Proves that in every $r$-connected graph with $r\geq 2$, any two longest cycles meet in at least $\Omega(r^{5/8})$ vertices, improving on the bound of $\Omega(r^{3/5})$ from Chen, Faudree and Gould (1998).
notes: Labeled as Conjecture 1.2 in the paper; cited as Conjecture 5.2 in Grötschel [28] (1984) and Conjecture 4.15 in Bondy [10] (1995); proof uses combinatorial arguments and computer assistance.
paper_title: Longest cycles in vertex-transitive and highly connected graphs
